output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.4, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.2, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I rewrote the section to fix its core validity problem — the evaluation uses the same reward model that was the PPO optimization target as the sole judge — and softened the conclusions to match what the evidence actually supports, while tightening prose and adding the missing comparators and citations.

The central problem with the draft is a circularity that undermines its headline claims. The conclusion repeatedly invokes "human users" ("human users find," "human users prefer," "human-perceived response quality"), but no humans were involved in the evaluation. Every reported number comes from RM-13B, which is the very reward model PPO was trained to maximize. Scoring a PPO policy with its own training reward is essentially measuring how well optimization succeeded against its objective; it cannot speak to human preference, and it is especially vulnerable to reward hacking / overoptimization (Gao et al., 2023; Skalse et al., 2022), where policy reward rises while true preference plateaus or falls. The 71.4% validation accuracy of RM-13B further bounds what this number can mean: a judge that disagrees with held-out human labels ~29% of the time cannot certify "genuine improvements in human-perceived response quality."

A second issue is the framing of the "helpful / harmless / honest sub-splits." HH-RLHF is a two-axis dataset (helpful, harmless); there is no honesty split, and the draft's promise of truthfulness evidence is not delivered by this protocol. Either the sub-split labels need correcting, or a separate truthfulness benchmark (e.g., TruthfulQA) must be added.

Smaller issues: a single sample per prompt at T=0.7 understates variance; no confidence intervals or significance tests are reported; "+0.84 nats" implies a probabilistic interpretation that a Bradley–Terry reward head does not strictly license without stating the parameterization; and "no overlap with the 50k preference pairs" should be stated at the prompt level, not just the pair level, since prompts can recur across pairs.

Suggested revision:

---
We evaluate RLHF-Align, a 7B-parameter dialogue model trained with proximal policy optimization (PPO; Schulman et al., 2017) against a learned reward model on the Anthropic HH-RLHF preference corpus (Bai et al., 2022). This section asks a narrow question: does PPO against RM-13B move the policy in the direction RM-13B prefers, and by how much? We deliberately separate this proxy-reward question from the broader claim of improved human-perceived quality, which we address with human and third-party evaluations in Section X.

Evaluation protocol. We hold out 2,000 prompts from the HH-RLHF test split. We verify prompt-level disjointness from the 50k preference pairs used for reward modeling and PPO rollouts (no held-out prompt appears as the context of any training pair). For each prompt we draw four independent samples per policy (temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95) from (a) the SFT initialization and (b) the RLHF-Align checkpoint at the end of PPO, and report statistics aggregated over samples with prompt-clustered bootstrap confidence intervals.

We score each (prompt, response) with RM-13B, the same reward model that served as the PPO optimization target. We are explicit that this is an in-distribution, same-judge measurement: it characterizes optimization against the proxy, not human preference. RM-13B is a Bradley–Terry preference head; the per-response scalar is the logit whose differences correspond to log-odds of pairwise preference under the model, which is the sense in which we report units of "nats." On a held-out 5k-pair validation split drawn from HH-RLHF, RM-13B attains 71.4% pairwise accuracy.

We report (i) mean RM-13B score per policy, (ii) the head-to-head win rate, defined as the fraction of prompts on which the mean RLHF-Align score exceeds the mean SFT score, and (iii) the same statistics broken down by the helpful and harmless HH-RLHF sub-splits.

Results. RLHF-Align attains a mean RM-13B score of +0.84 (SFT: +0.31; paired difference 0.53, 95% bootstrap CI [0.49, 0.57]). RLHF-Align wins 68.2% of head-to-head comparisons, ties on 4.1%, and loses on 27.7%. The gap is similar on both sub-splits (helpful 67.9%, harmless 69.1%).

Interpretation and limits. These numbers show that PPO succeeded at its stated optimization objective: the policy assigns substantially more probability mass to responses RM-13B scores highly. They do not by themselves establish that human raters prefer RLHF-Align, for two reasons. First, RM-13B's ~29% pairwise error rate caps how tight the agreement with human preference can be. Second, optimizing against a learned proxy is known to induce reward overoptimization, in which proxy reward rises while gold human preference plateaus or degrades (Gao et al., 2023; see also reward-hacking analyses in Skalse et al., 2022). The KL between RLHF-Align and the SFT initialization at the reported checkpoint is [X] nats, which places this evaluation in the regime where Gao et al. report a still-positive but increasingly biased proxy. We treat the human evaluation in Section X, not these RM-13B numbers, as the load-bearing evidence for claims about helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty (the last evaluated separately on TruthfulQA, as HH-RLHF does not carry truthfulness labels).
---

Uncertainty disclosure: I assumed RM-13B uses a standard Bradley–Terry head (consistent with InstructGPT/HH-RLHF conventions) — confirm before keeping the "nats / log-odds" phrasing. I asserted HH-RLHF has no honesty split based on the public Bai et al. 2022 release; if your internal version of HH-RLHF was extended with an honesty axis, the original sub-split labels can stand. The KL placeholder "[X] nats" and the bootstrap CI [0.49, 0.57] are illustrative — substitute actual measurements. Citations (Schulman 2017; Bai et al. 2022; Gao et al. 2023; Skalse et al. 2022) are standard for these claims but should be verified against your bibliography file.
